options: At the end with negation only
questions.explanation: Structure: [subject] + [verb] + graag. For negation: 'Ik wandel niet graag.' The 'niet' goes before 'graag'.

questions.q: 'Je zwemt graag' — what happens in the yes/no question form?
options: Zwem je graag? (-t drops in inversion)
questions.explanation: Inversion rule: when 'je/jij' comes AFTER the verb, the -t drops. 'zwem je' not 'zwemt je'.

questions.q: How do you say 'She likes cycling'?
questions.explanation: 3rd person singular = verb stem + t. 'fietsen' → stem 'fiets' + t = 'fietst'. Zij fietst graag.

questions.q: 'Ga je mee fietsen?' — what does 'mee' add here?
options: It means 'along/with' — you're inviting them to join
It is the separable verb prefix meaning 'away'
questions.explanation: 'meegaan' (to go along) is a separable verb. 'mee' flies to the end in main clauses.

questions.q: What is the most natural way to accept an invitation in Belgian Dutch?
questions.explanation: 'Ja, graag!' is the most natural and versatile acceptance. It literally means 'Yes, gladly!' Very Belgian!

questions.q: 'Ik heb geen zin' — when would you use this?
options: When you don't feel like it
questions.explanation: 'geen zin hebben' = to not feel like it / to not be in the mood.

questions.q: You're declining an invitation. Which response sounds most Belgian?
options: Ik heb geen tijd. Volgende keer!
questions.explanation: Belgians always soften a refusal: reason + softener ('Volgende keer!' / 'Misschien een andere keer?'). A bare 'nee' sounds rude.

questions.q: 'Ga je met mij mee wandelen?' — what does 'mij' add?
options: It makes the sentence past tense
It specifies you're going WITH a particular person (me)
It changes the verb from 'gaan' to 'meegaan'
questions.explanation: 'Ga je mee?' = Are you coming? 'Ga je met mij mee?' = Are you coming WITH ME? 'met + pronoun + mee' sandwiches the companion.
